subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Expanded National Government Powers and Policy Making
stimulus:
The United States Constitution establishes a federal system of government. Under federalism, policy making is shared between national and state governments. Over time, the powers of the national government have increased relative to those of the state governments.

Develop an argument about whether the expanded powers of the national government benefits or hinders policy making.

Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents:
• The Articles of Confederation
• Brutus 1
• The Federalist 10

In your essay, you must:
✓ Articulate a defensible claim or thesis that responds to the prompt and establishes a line of reasoning.
✓ Support your claim or thesis with at least TWO pieces of accurate and relevant evidence.
   • One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed above.
   • A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document not used as your first piece of evidence, or it may be from your knowledge of course concepts.
✓ Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
✓ Respond to an opposing or alternative perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.
parts.scoring_notes: The thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. It does NOT need to reference foundational documents — that requirement applies to the evidence row only. The thesis may be located anywhere in the response. A 'because' clause with at least one reason constitutes a line of reasoning.

scoring_points.description: Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row A: Articulate a defensible claim or thesis that responds to the prompt and establishes a line of reasoning. (1 point) The claim or thesis responds to the prompt rather than restating or rephrasing it and establishes a line of reasoning. Examples that earn this point: 'The expanded powers of the national government have made it more efficient, and if states had more power it would be dangerous.' 'The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because of the strength of the Constitution, the increase of cooperative federalism, and the advantages of fiscal federalism.' Examples that do NOT earn this point: 'Because of my knowledge of the United States Government, I believe the expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making.' (No line of reasoning.) 'The federal government has expanded power over time.' (Does not respond to the prompt.)

required_elements: Takes a clear position on whether expanded national power benefits or hinders policy making
Establishes a line of reasoning (provides at least one reason via 'because' clause or equivalent)
alternatives.correct_example: The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because it allows for more consistency across states and makes it easier to respond to large scale problems.

required_elements: Takes a clear position on whether expanded national power benefits or hinders policy making
Establishes a line of reasoning (provides at least one reason via 'because' clause or equivalent)
alternatives.correct_example: The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because of the strength of the Constitution, the increase of cooperative federalism, and the advantages of fiscal federalism.

wrong_examples: The federal government has expanded power over time. (Factual statement, no position on benefits/hinders)
Because of my knowledge of United States Government, I believe the expanded powers benefit policy making. (No line of reasoning — just assertion)
There are pros and cons to expanded national power. (No clear position taken)
common_traps: Do NOT require foundational documents in the thesis — documents are only required in the evidence row
A simple 'because X and Y' clause IS a line of reasoning — do not require sophistication
Restating the prompt without adding reasoning does not earn the point

sample_responses.response: The expanded powers of the national government have made it more efficient, and if states had more power it would be dangerous.
sample_responses.commentary: Takes a clear position (expanded national power = beneficial) and establishes a line of reasoning (efficiency and dangers of state power). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because of the strength of the Constitution, the increase of cooperative federalism, and the advantages of fiscal federalism.
sample_responses.commentary: Clear position with multiple lines of reasoning provided. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Because of my knowledge of United States Government, I believe the expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making.
sample_responses.commentary: States a position but provides no line of reasoning — no 'because' clause explaining why the expanded powers are beneficial. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of evidence. One must come from a listed foundational document (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10). A second piece can come from any foundational document or course concept.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: The student earns 0, 1, 2, OR 3 points — award the HIGHEST tier met, not each independently. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A (thesis). Tier 3 requires one piece from a listed foundational document.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 1 point: Provides one piece of evidence that is relevant to the topic of the prompt. May or may not have a claim or thesis.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic (does not need to support the thesis directly)
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Federalist 10, the Commerce Clause, or another relevant concept.

wrong_examples: Evidence is inaccurate or completely irrelevant to the prompt topic
common_traps: At tier 1, evidence only needs to be relevant — it does not need to directly support the thesis

sample_responses.response: The Federalist 10 discusses factions and how a large republic can better control them.
sample_responses.commentary: Mentions a foundational document (Federalist 10) relevant to the topic of national power and policy making. Earns Tier 1.

sample_responses.response: The Civil War showed that states can disagree with the federal government.
sample_responses.commentary: Not sufficiently connected to the prompt about national government powers and policy making — more historical context than course concept evidence. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. (Requires Row A thesis earned.)
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 2 points: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim or thesis. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts. To earn 2 or 3 points in Row B, the response must have a defensible claim or thesis (earned the point in Row A). Example that earns 2 points: 'The U.S. has already tried giving states more power than the national government, and it has proved to be ineffective. The Articles of Confederation are a prime example of why a strong national government is better. With the Articles, the government could do very little. It made the states more independent by allowing them to have their own currency and impose their own taxes.'

required_elements: One specific piece of evidence that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces of evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 10 to argue that a large republic controls factions, supporting the claim that expanded national power benefits policy making.

wrong_examples: Lists evidence without connecting it to the thesis
common_traps: This tier requires Row A (thesis) — if thesis is 0, cap evidence at tier 1

sample_responses.response: The U.S. has already tried giving states more power than the national government, and it has proved to be ineffective. The Articles of Confederation are a prime example of why a strong national government is better. With the Articles, the government could do very little. It made the states more independent by allowing them to have their own currency and impose their own taxes.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the Articles of Confederation as specific evidence supporting the thesis that expanded national power benefits policy making. Earns Tier 2.

sample_responses.response: Federalist 10 talks about factions. The national government has also used the Commerce Clause.
sample_responses.commentary: Names two pieces of evidence but neither is connected to the thesis — evidence must support the claim, not just be relevant. Earns at most Tier 1. 0 pts for Tier 2.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, at least one from a listed foundational document. (Requires Row A thesis earned.)
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 3 points: Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. To earn 3 points, the response must use one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt. Example that earns 3 points: 'According to Federalist 70, a strong executive benefits the country because it is easier for one person than a group to make decisions, and someone is held accountable.'

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one piece from Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Federalist 10 to argue large republics control factions AND uses the Commerce Clause to show how national regulation creates uniform economic policy — both supporting the thesis.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence mentioned but neither from a listed foundational document
Evidence from foundational document but does not support the thesis
common_traps: The foundational document must be from the LISTED documents (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10)
Both pieces must specifically support the thesis, not just be relevant to the topic

sample_responses.response: According to Federalist 70, a strong executive benefits the country because it is easier for one person than a group to make decisions, and someone is held accountable. Additionally, the Commerce Clause gives the national government authority to create uniform economic policy across all states.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses Federalist 70 (a listed foundational document) plus the Commerce Clause (a course concept), both supporting the thesis. Earns Tier 3.

sample_responses.response: The Commerce Clause gives Congress power over interstate trade. The Necessary and Proper Clause also expands national authority.
sample_responses.commentary: Two course concepts but neither is from the listed foundational documents (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, Federalist 10). Caps at Tier 2. 0 pts for Tier 3.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis) and at least 2 points in Row B (evidence). Must explain HOW or WHY the evidence supports the claim — not just restate it.

scoring_points.description: Reasoning: Use reasoning to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row C: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis. (1 point) Must explain the relationship between the evidence provided and the claim or thesis. Examples that earn this point: 'The Articles of Confederation made it harder to pass laws because each state has very different agendas, and it required over a majority of them to agree. To amend, it required a unanimous decision. This makes it nearly impossible to add a new amendment to change policy.' 'This efficiency and accountability from a strong executive benefits policy making.' To earn this point, the response must have Row A (thesis) and at least 2 points in Row B (evidence).

required_elements: Explains the logical connection between evidence and thesis (how or why)
Goes beyond restating the evidence — must show cause-effect or analytical reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The Articles of Confederation made it harder to pass laws because each state had different agendas and required a supermajority to agree, making it nearly impossible to change policy. This shows that expanded national power improves policy making by reducing gridlock.

wrong_examples: Simply listing evidence without explaining the connection to the thesis
Restating the thesis without using evidence to support it
common_traps: Requires Row A (thesis) and at least tier 2 evidence (Row B >= 2pts)
The explanation must connect at least one piece of evidence to the claim

sample_responses.response: The Articles of Confederation made it harder to pass laws because each state has very different agendas, and it required over a majority of them to agree. To amend, it required a unanimous decision. This makes it nearly impossible to change policy — showing why expanded national power is beneficial for policy making.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the causal chain from Articles of Confederation facts to the thesis claim about expanded national power benefiting policy making. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: This efficiency and accountability from a strong executive benefits policy making.
sample_responses.commentary: Brief but sufficient — explains the logical link between the evidence (strong executive) and the thesis (benefits policy making). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The evidence I used shows that the national government has more power now than before.
sample_responses.commentary: Restates the evidence without explaining the causal or logical connection to the thesis. Does not show WHY the evidence supports the claim. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternative perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis). Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut, refute, or concede it. Simply identifying an opposing view without responding to it does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Alternate Perspective: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row D: Respond to an opposing or alternative perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. (1 point) Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut that perspective. Example that earns this point: 'Some people may argue that the federal government is too large, and that states are best suited to address the needs of their people without interference from the federal government. However, this is not true. The state governments are not equipped to handle all of the problems they face without the federal government. The federal government can act with uniformity to affect all states, to ensure everyone is guaranteed the same protections as everyone else.' Responses that do NOT earn this point: Restate the opposite of the claim or thesis; identify or describe an alternate perspective but do not refute, concede, or rebut it; refute a foundational document rather than an alternate perspective. Requires Row A (thesis).

required_elements: Describes an alternate or opposing perspective
Rebuts, refutes, or concedes the opposing perspective with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some argue that expanded national power hinders policy making because it ignores local needs, as Brutus 1 warns. However, the system of federalism still allows states to make policies in areas like education, so the national government's expanded power supplements rather than replaces state authority.

wrong_examples: Some people disagree. (Acknowledges opposition without rebutting)
The opposite of my thesis is wrong. (No actual engagement with the opposing argument)
common_traps: Requires Row A (thesis) — if thesis is 0, rebuttal cannot earn the point
Must do MORE than just identify the opposing view — must respond to it

sample_responses.response: Some people may argue that the federal government is too large, and that states are best suited to address the needs of their people without interference from the federal government. However, this is not true. The state governments are not equipped to handle all of the problems they face without the federal government. The federal government can act with uniformity to affect all states, to ensure everyone is guaranteed the same protections as everyone else.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the opposing view (states best suited to address local needs) and rebuts it by arguing the federal government provides uniform protections that states cannot. Engages substantively with the counterargument. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: In Brutus 1, the author argues expanded national power could hurt states and make policies less responsive to local needs. Even though this is a concern, the national government is still important because it can create laws that apply to everyone and keep the country unified.
sample_responses.commentary: Concede-plus-generic-reaffirm. The rebuttal restates why national power matters (unity, universal laws) but does NOT address the SPECIFIC concern about local responsiveness. Generic reasons that pivot away from the critique do not constitute engagement with the opposing perspective. 0 pts.

sample_responses.response: Critics like Brutus 1 argue expanded national power hinders policy making because it ignores local needs. However, federalism still reserves significant powers to states via the 10th Amendment in areas like education — so states retain authority to respond to local needs, and national expansion supplements rather than replaces state authority.
sample_responses.commentary: True concede-plus-reaffirm: engages the SPECIFIC local-needs concern by explaining how federalism and the 10th Amendment preserve state capacity for local responsiveness. The rebuttal directly answers the critique rather than pivoting to generic national-power benefits.
